1:
"If there be a controversy between men, and they come unto judgment, that the judges may judge them; then they shall justify the righteous, and condemn the wicked." The witnesses note the office of the judge — to justify the righteous and condemn the wicked in a controversy. The witnesses note that Gill notes a controversy between two or more brought to judgment. The witnesses note that Henry marks the accuser and accused brought face to face before the judges, that the cause be tried. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a controversy — brought to judgment; the the judges may judge — a fair trial; the justify the righteous — acquit the innocent; the condemn the wicked — the great duty of judgment (Prov. 17:15).

The witnesses agree the verse states the very end of justice — to discern and declare the right: the judges were to "justify the righteous, and condemn the wicked," giving to each his due upon a fair hearing; the order of bringing accuser and accused before the bench shows the necessity of a just trial, and the charge to acquit the innocent and condemn the guilty marks the great duty of judgment; to do the contrary — to justify the wicked or condemn the righteous — is itself "an abomination to the LORD" (Prov. 17:15), so that the judge bears a sacred trust to declare the truth and uphold the right.

So the witnesses make the verse justify the righteous: The witnesses note the a controversy: brought to judgment. The witnesses note the the judges may judge: a fair trial. The witnesses note the justify the righteous: acquit the innocent. The witnesses note the condemn the wicked: the great duty of judgment (Prov. 17:15). The expositors observe the a controversy: brought to judgment. The expositors observe the the judges may judge: a fair trial. The expositors observe the justify the righteous: acquit the innocent. The expositors observe the condemn the wicked: the great duty of judgment (Prov. 17:15). The believer is taught to mark the a controversy, the the judges may judge, the justify the righteous, the condemn the wicked, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the judges were to justify the righteous and condemn the wicked — the very end of justice, a sacred trust to declare the truth, the contrary an abomination to the LORD (Prov. 17:15).
2:
"And it shall be, if the wicked man be worthy to be beaten, that the judge shall cause him to lie down, and to be beaten before his face, according to his fault, by a certain number." The witnesses note the guilty beaten in the judge's presence, by a fixed number proportioned to the offence. The witnesses note that Gill notes there were lesser crimes than capital ones punished with beating or scourging, set out at large by the Jews. The witnesses note that the beating was before the judge's face, by a certain number. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the worthy to be beaten — the guilty sentenced; the before his face — in the judge's presence; the according to his fault — the stripes proportioned; the measure and mercy — punishment kept within bounds (Isa. 27:8).

The witnesses agree the punishment was measured, witnessed, and proportioned: the guilty was beaten "before his face" — in the judge's very presence, that it be neither excessive nor cruel — and "according to his fault, by a certain number," the stripes fitted to the offence; the law thus guarded even the punishment of the wicked from passion and abuse, the judge himself overseeing it; it teaches that justice is to be exact and humane, the penalty matched to the crime and kept within bounds, that even in chastisement mercy and measure rule (cf. Isa. 27:8, in measure... thou wilt debate with it).

So the witnesses make the verse measured punishment: The witnesses note the worthy to be beaten: the guilty sentenced. The witnesses note the before his face: in the judge's presence. The witnesses note the according to his fault: the stripes proportioned. The witnesses note the measure and mercy: punishment kept within bounds (Isa. 27:8). The expositors observe the worthy to be beaten: the guilty sentenced. The expositors observe the before his face: in the judge's presence. The expositors observe the according to his fault: the stripes proportioned. The expositors observe the measure and mercy: punishment kept within bounds (Isa. 27:8). The believer is taught to mark the worthy to be beaten, the before his face, the according to his fault, the measure and mercy, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the guilty was beaten before the judge, by a number proportioned to his fault — punishment measured, witnessed, and humane, even chastisement ruled by measure and mercy (Isa. 27:8).
3:
"Forty stripes he may give him, and not exceed: lest, if he should exceed, and beat him above these with many stripes, then thy brother should seem vile unto thee." The witnesses note the limit of forty stripes, lest the brother be degraded by an excessive beating. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jewish canons fixed it at thirty-nine, "forty save one," that the number be not exceeded. The witnesses note that the limit was set lest the brother seem vile. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the forty stripes — the fixed limit; the not exceed — lest the brother be degraded; the forty save one — the Jews' careful thirty-nine (2 Cor. 11:24); the dignity guarded — the guilty still a brother.

The witnesses agree the cap on the stripes guarded the dignity of even the guilty as a "brother": the beating might not "exceed" forty, lest the man "seem vile" — degraded below the standing of a fellow Israelite; the Jews, to be sure of not breaking the limit, gave but thirty-nine, "forty save one" — the very number the apostle Paul received five times (2 Cor. 11:24); the law teaches that punishment is not to destroy a man's dignity, that even the wicked, being a brother, is to be chastened without contempt, justice tempered by a regard for the humanity of the punished.

So the witnesses make the verse forty stripes, no more: The witnesses note the forty stripes: the fixed limit. The witnesses note the not exceed: lest the brother be degraded. The witnesses note the forty save one: the Jews' careful thirty-nine (2 Cor. 11:24). The witnesses note the dignity guarded: the guilty still a brother. The expositors observe the forty stripes: the fixed limit. The expositors observe the not exceed: lest the brother be degraded. The expositors observe the forty save one: the Jews' careful thirty-nine (2 Cor. 11:24). The expositors observe the dignity guarded: the guilty still a brother. The believer is taught to mark the forty stripes, the not exceed, the forty save one, the dignity guarded, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the stripes might not exceed forty, lest the brother seem vile — the dignity of even the guilty guarded, the apostle himself receiving forty save one (2 Cor. 11:24), justice tempered by regard for the punished.
4:
"Thou shalt not muzzle the ox when he treadeth out the corn." The witnesses note the law that the ox treading out the grain was not to be muzzled from eating. The witnesses note that Gill notes the ox, used in treading out corn as in ploughing, might not be restrained by a muzzle from eating as it had opportunity. The witnesses note that it was to be free to eat of the corn it laboured in. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not muzzle the ox — free to eat as it works; the treadeth out the corn — sharing in its labour; the mercy to the beast — kindness to the animal; the the labourer's due — the worker worthy of his reward (1 Cor. 9:9).

The witnesses agree this kindly law toward the beast carries a far-reaching principle: the ox that trod the grain was to share in it, not muzzled while it laboured; the mercy to the animal teaches the justice owed to every labourer — that he who works should partake of the fruit of his work; the apostle twice applies this verse to the maintenance of gospel ministers, "doth God take care for oxen? or saith he it altogether for our sakes?" (1 Cor. 9:9; 1 Tim. 5:18), drawing from the unmuzzled ox the rule that "the labourer is worthy of his reward," God's care reaching from the beast to the servant of his church.

So the witnesses make the verse the unmuzzled ox: The witnesses note the not muzzle the ox: free to eat as it works. The witnesses note the treadeth out the corn: sharing in its labour. The witnesses note the mercy to the beast: kindness to the animal. The witnesses note the the labourer's due: the worker worthy of his reward (1 Cor. 9:9). The expositors observe the not muzzle the ox: free to eat as it works. The expositors observe the treadeth out the corn: sharing in its labour. The expositors observe the mercy to the beast: kindness to the animal. The expositors observe the the labourer's due: the worker worthy of his reward (1 Cor. 9:9). The believer is taught to mark the not muzzle the ox, the treadeth out the corn, the mercy to the beast, the the labourer's due, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the ox treading the grain was not to be muzzled — mercy to the beast teaching the justice owed every labourer, the apostle drawing from it that the worker is worthy of his reward (1 Cor. 9:9; 1 Tim. 5:18).
5:
"If brethren dwell together, and one of them die, and have no child, the wife of the dead shall not marry without unto a stranger: her husband's brother shall... take her to him to wife..." The witnesses note the law of levirate marriage — the brother to wed the widow of one who died childless. The witnesses note that Gill notes "brethren dwell together" means in the same house, brought up together, one being married and the others living with him. The witnesses note that the brother was to take her to wife and raise up seed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brethren dwell together — in one house; the one die childless — the line in danger; the the brother take her — to raise up seed; the the kinsman-redeemer — boaz, pointing to Christ (Ruth 4:10).

The witnesses agree the levirate law provided for the continuance of a dead brother's name and line: where a man died childless, his brother was to marry the widow and "raise up seed" to the deceased, that his name not perish from Israel; the law breathes a tender care for the family and its memory, and a brotherly duty to the dead; it lies at the heart of the book of Ruth, where Boaz the kinsman-redeemer fulfils it, and so points to Christ, the true Kinsman who raises up seed and preserves a people to the name of God, redeeming the inheritance that was lost (cf. Ruth 4:10).

So the witnesses make the verse levirate marriage: The witnesses note the brethren dwell together: in one house. The witnesses note the one die childless: the line in danger. The witnesses note the the brother take her: to raise up seed. The witnesses note the the kinsman-redeemer: Boaz, pointing to Christ (Ruth 4:10). The expositors observe the brethren dwell together: in one house. The expositors observe the one die childless: the line in danger. The expositors observe the the brother take her: to raise up seed. The expositors observe the the kinsman-redeemer: Boaz, pointing to Christ (Ruth 4:10). The believer is taught to mark the brethren dwell together, the one die childless, the the brother take her, the the kinsman-redeemer, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the brother was to marry the childless widow and raise up seed to the dead — a tender care for the family name, fulfilled in Boaz the kinsman-redeemer and pointing to Christ who preserves a people to God (Ruth 4:10).
6:
"And it shall be, that the firstborn which she beareth shall succeed in the name of his brother which is dead, that his name be not put out of Israel." The witnesses note the firstborn of the levirate union reckoned to the dead brother, that his name be not blotted out. The witnesses note that Gill notes the firstborn she bore to her husband's brother. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him succeeding in the name of the dead, that his name be not put out of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the firstborn — of the levirate union; the succeed in the dead's name — reckoned to him; the not put out of Israel — his line continued; the Christ shall see his seed — a people preserved to God (Isa. 53:10).

The witnesses agree the verse names the gracious purpose of the law — the preservation of a name and inheritance in Israel: the firstborn of the new union was reckoned to the dead brother, "that his name be not put out of Israel," his line and memory continued; the care taken that no man's name should perish from among God's people reveals how God values the place of each in his covenant nation, and the family's share in the promised land; it shadows the gospel, in which Christ raises up to himself a seed that shall not be cut off, preserving a people whose names are written in heaven (cf. Isa. 53:10, he shall see his seed).

So the witnesses make the verse the name preserved: The witnesses note the the firstborn: of the levirate union. The witnesses note the succeed in the dead's name: reckoned to him. The witnesses note the not put out of Israel: his line continued. The witnesses note the Christ shall see his seed: a people preserved to God (Isa. 53:10). The expositors observe the the firstborn: of the levirate union. The expositors observe the succeed in the dead's name: reckoned to him. The expositors observe the not put out of Israel: his line continued. The expositors observe the Christ shall see his seed: a people preserved to God (Isa. 53:10). The believer is taught to mark the the firstborn, the succeed in the dead's name, the not put out of Israel, the Christ shall see his seed, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the firstborn was reckoned to the dead, that his name be not blotted from Israel — God valuing each name in his covenant, shadowing Christ who raises up a seed that shall not be cut off (Isa. 53:10).
7:
"And if the man like not to take his brother's wife, then let his brother's wife go up to the gate unto the elders, and say, My husband's brother refuseth to raise up unto his brother a name in Israel..." The witnesses note the widow's recourse if the brother refused — to bring the matter to the elders at the gate. The witnesses note that Gill notes the provision for when the brother refused, as Onan would have done had this been law then. The witnesses note that the widow was to go to the elders and declare his refusal. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the man like not — refusing his duty; the go up to the elders — the matter brought to the gate; the declare his refusal — calling him to account; the the widow's redress — the helpless given a voice (Luke 18:3).

The witnesses agree the law gave the widow a remedy and the duty a public sanction: if the brother "like not" to do his part, the widow might bring her complaint "to the gate unto the elders," that he be called to account; the recourse shows God's care that the helpless widow not be left without redress, and that the duty to the dead be enforced by the community; though the man could not be compelled to marry against his will, he could not refuse without public reckoning — the obligation to the family upheld, and the widow given a voice before the judges (cf. Luke 18:3, avenge me of mine adversary).

So the witnesses make the verse the widow's recourse: The witnesses note the the man like not: refusing his duty. The witnesses note the go up to the elders: the matter brought to the gate. The witnesses note the declare his refusal: calling him to account. The witnesses note the the widow's redress: the helpless given a voice (Luke 18:3). The expositors observe the the man like not: refusing his duty. The expositors observe the go up to the elders: the matter brought to the gate. The expositors observe the declare his refusal: calling him to account. The expositors observe the the widow's redress: the helpless given a voice (Luke 18:3). The believer is taught to mark the the man like not, the go up to the elders, the declare his refusal, the the widow's redress, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the widow might bring the refusing brother before the elders — God giving the helpless widow redress and the duty a public sanction, the obligation to the family upheld before the judges (Luke 18:3).
8:
"Then the elders of his city shall call him, and speak unto him: and if he stand to it, and say, I like not to take her;" The witnesses note the elders calling and reasoning with the man, who yet may persist in his refusal. The witnesses note that Gill notes the elders were to require him to come, recite the law, explain it, exhort him to comply or show reason, and have his final resolution. The witnesses note that he might stand to it and say, I like not to take her. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the elders shall call — requiring him to come; the speak unto him — reciting and urging the law; the if he stand to it — his free refusal allowed; the urged, not forced — the will left free (Philem. 14).

The witnesses agree the elders' part was to admonish and persuade, not to compel: they were to "call him" and "speak unto him," reciting the law and urging him to do his duty, yet if he persisted — "if he stand to it" — his free refusal was allowed, with the consequence to follow; the law thus joined moral suasion to liberty, pressing the duty with all earnestness yet leaving the choice with the man, that he act of his own will and bear the shame of his refusal; it teaches that duty is to be urged but not forced, the conscience appealed to and the will left free (cf. Philem. 14, that thy benefit should not be of necessity, but willingly).

So the witnesses make the verse the elders reason: The witnesses note the the elders shall call: requiring him to come. The witnesses note the speak unto him: reciting and urging the law. The witnesses note the if he stand to it: his free refusal allowed. The witnesses note the urged, not forced: the will left free (Philem. 14). The expositors observe the the elders shall call: requiring him to come. The expositors observe the speak unto him: reciting and urging the law. The expositors observe the if he stand to it: his free refusal allowed. The expositors observe the urged, not forced: the will left free (Philem. 14). The believer is taught to mark the the elders shall call, the speak unto him, the if he stand to it, the urged, not forced, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the elders called and reasoned with the man, yet his refusal stood if he persisted — duty urged but not forced, the conscience appealed to and the will left free (Philem. 14).
9:
"Then shall his brother's wife come unto him in the presence of the elders, and loose his shoe from off his foot, and spit in his face... So shall it be done unto that man that will not build up his brother's house." The witnesses note the public shaming of the refuser — his shoe loosed and the spitting before the elders. The witnesses note that Gill notes the time and place appointed beforehand, the woman apprised, coming for the ceremony. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the difficulty of finding the reason of these rites, marks of contempt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the loose his shoe — the token of a surrendered right; the spit in his face — the mark of contempt; the before the elders — a public disgrace; the selfish neglect shamed — who builds not his brother's house (Ruth 4:7).

The witnesses agree the rite branded the refuser with public disgrace for failing his brother's house: the widow loosed his shoe and spat before him, signs of contempt, "so... done unto that man that will not build up his brother's house"; the loosing of the shoe — elsewhere the token of giving up a right (cf. Ruth 4:7) — marked his surrender of the kinsman's claim, and the spitting his shame; the law thus visited a real but bloodless penalty on selfish neglect of duty, that the man who would not love his dead brother's memory bear the reproach of his hardness before all.

So the witnesses make the verse the shame of refusal: The witnesses note the loose his shoe: the token of a surrendered right. The witnesses note the spit in his face: the mark of contempt. The witnesses note the before the elders: a public disgrace. The witnesses note the selfish neglect shamed: who builds not his brother's house (Ruth 4:7). The expositors observe the loose his shoe: the token of a surrendered right. The expositors observe the spit in his face: the mark of contempt. The expositors observe the before the elders: a public disgrace. The expositors observe the selfish neglect shamed: who builds not his brother's house (Ruth 4:7). The believer is taught to mark the loose his shoe, the spit in his face, the before the elders, the selfish neglect shamed, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the refuser's shoe was loosed and he spat upon before the elders — a public shame on selfish neglect of duty, the man who would not build his brother's house bearing the reproach of his hardness (Ruth 4:7).
10:
"And his name shall be called in Israel, The house of him that hath his shoe loosed." The witnesses note the lasting reproach — his family marked by the name "the house of the loosed shoe." The witnesses note that Gill notes it was not his personal name but his family that was so called, a mark of infamy resting on his house as well. The witnesses note that this reproach was set in Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his name called — his family so marked; the the house of the loosed shoe — a standing reproach; the a mark of infamy — resting on the household; the neglect a lasting stain — unkindness branded with reproach (Prov. 10:7).

The witnesses agree the disgrace was made lasting and public, cleaving even to the man's house: his family was thenceforth called "the house of him that hath his shoe loosed," a standing reproach for his refusal; the abiding name shows how seriously the neglect of brotherly duty was regarded, the shame outliving the moment and marking the household; it teaches that the failure of love and duty leaves a lasting stain, and that God would have selfishness toward the brethren branded with reproach, the memory of unkindness preserved as a warning to others (cf. Prov. 10:7, the name of the wicked shall rot).

So the witnesses make the verse the lasting reproach: The witnesses note the his name called: his family so marked. The witnesses note the the house of the loosed shoe: a standing reproach. The witnesses note the a mark of infamy: resting on the household. The witnesses note the neglect a lasting stain: unkindness branded with reproach (Prov. 10:7). The expositors observe the his name called: his family so marked. The expositors observe the the house of the loosed shoe: a standing reproach. The expositors observe the a mark of infamy: resting on the household. The expositors observe the neglect a lasting stain: unkindness branded with reproach (Prov. 10:7). The believer is taught to mark the his name called, the the house of the loosed shoe, the a mark of infamy, the neglect a lasting stain, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that his house was called "the house of the loosed shoe" — a lasting reproach on neglect of brotherly duty, the shame outliving the moment, selfishness toward the brethren branded with reproach (Prov. 10:7).
11:
"When men strive together one with another, and the wife of the one draweth near for to deliver her husband out of the hand of him that smiteth him, and putteth forth her hand, and taketh him by the secrets:" The witnesses note the case of a woman who, in her husband's quarrel, immodestly seizes his adversary. The witnesses note that Gill notes the men strive and come to blows, contending for mastery. The witnesses note that Calvin notes this law, though apparently harsh, shows how pleasing modesty is to God and how he abominates indecency. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the men strive together — a quarrel come to blows; the the wife draws near — to deliver her husband; the takes him by the secrets — an act of gross immodesty; the the end no excuse — modesty pleasing to God (1 Tim. 2:9).

The reformed reader (Calvin) draws the lesson from a hard case: a woman, however zealous to rescue her husband, was not to defend him by an act of gross immodesty; the law, "apparently harsh," is meant to show "how very pleasing to God is modesty" and how he "abominates indecency"; even a good motive — to deliver her husband — did not excuse a shameful deed; the case teaches that the end does not justify an immodest means, and that purity and decency are to be preserved even in the heat of conflict, God valuing modesty so highly that its breach, however provoked, is condemned (cf. 1 Tim. 2:9).

So the witnesses make the verse an immodest act: The witnesses note the men strive together: a quarrel come to blows. The witnesses note the the wife draws near: to deliver her husband. The witnesses note the takes him by the secrets: an act of gross immodesty. The witnesses note the the end no excuse: modesty pleasing to God (1 Tim. 2:9). The expositors observe the men strive together: a quarrel come to blows. The expositors observe the the wife draws near: to deliver her husband. The expositors observe the takes him by the secrets: an act of gross immodesty. The expositors observe the the end no excuse: modesty pleasing to God (1 Tim. 2:9). The believer is taught to mark the men strive together, the the wife draws near, the takes him by the secrets, the the end no excuse, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that a woman was not to defend her husband by an immodest seizing of his foe — the end no excuse for a shameful deed, God valuing modesty so highly that its breach, however provoked, is condemned (1 Tim. 2:9).
12:
"Then thou shalt cut off her hand, thine eye shall not pity her." The witnesses note the severe penalty for the immodest act, to deter such shamelessness. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was done by the civil magistrate or his order, not the man or bystander; the severity to deter women from so immodest and injurious an action, and our Lord is thought to refer to it. The witnesses note that the eye was not to pity her. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the cut off her hand — the magistrate's sentence; the not in private revenge — by lawful order; the thine eye not pity — justice not softened; the cut off what offends — dealt with decisively (Matt. 5:30).

The witnesses agree the severity marked how gravely God regarded the breach of modesty and the injury done: the punishment was inflicted by the magistrate, not in private revenge, and "thine eye shall not pity her," that justice not be softened into license; the harshness, terrible to flesh, was a strong deterrent against shameless and injurious conduct; many take our Lord's word "if thy hand offend thee, cut it off" (Matt. 5:30) to allude to it — the principle that what leads to sin and shame must be dealt with decisively, modesty and purity guarded with unflinching seriousness.

So the witnesses make the verse cut off her hand: The witnesses note the cut off her hand: the magistrate's sentence. The witnesses note the not in private revenge: by lawful order. The witnesses note the thine eye not pity: justice not softened. The witnesses note the cut off what offends: dealt with decisively (Matt. 5:30). The expositors observe the cut off her hand: the magistrate's sentence. The expositors observe the not in private revenge: by lawful order. The expositors observe the thine eye not pity: justice not softened. The expositors observe the cut off what offends: dealt with decisively (Matt. 5:30). The believer is taught to mark the cut off her hand, the not in private revenge, the thine eye not pity, the cut off what offends, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that the immodest act met a severe, magistrate-imposed penalty — God regarding the breach of modesty gravely, a deterrent against shamelessness, what leads to sin dealt with decisively (Matt. 5:30).
13:
"Thou shalt not have in thy bag divers weights, a great and a small." The witnesses note the ban on carrying differing weights — a great and a small — for fraud in trade. The witnesses note that Gill notes weights were of stone in those times, "a stone and a stone," and were not to be different. The witnesses note that Henry sets the forbidding of unjust dealing by false weights. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the divers weights — a great and a small; the in thy bag — the trader's cheating tools; the fraud forbidden — no defrauding the neighbour; the honesty in trade — a just weight God's delight (Prov. 11:1).

The witnesses agree the law strikes at the common fraud of the dishonest trader: to keep "divers weights, a great and a small" — a heavy weight for buying and a light for selling — was a cheating of one's neighbour under a show of fairness; the ban guards honesty in business, that none defraud another in the very acts of daily commerce; God's concern reaching into the merchant's bag shows that true religion governs the market as well as the temple, and that "a false balance is abomination to the LORD: but a just weight is his delight" (Prov. 11:1), honesty in trade a part of holiness.

So the witnesses make the verse no divers weights: The witnesses note the divers weights: a great and a small. The witnesses note the in thy bag: the trader's cheating tools. The witnesses note the fraud forbidden: no defrauding the neighbour. The witnesses note the honesty in trade: a just weight God's delight (Prov. 11:1). The expositors observe the divers weights: a great and a small. The expositors observe the in thy bag: the trader's cheating tools. The expositors observe the fraud forbidden: no defrauding the neighbour. The expositors observe the honesty in trade: a just weight God's delight (Prov. 11:1). The believer is taught to mark the divers weights, the in thy bag, the fraud forbidden, the honesty in trade, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that no differing weights were to be carried for fraud — honesty in business guarded, religion governing the market as the temple, a just weight the delight of the LORD (Prov. 11:1).
14:
"Thou shalt not have in thine house divers measures, a great and a small." The witnesses note the same ban extended to measures — a great and a small — in the house. The witnesses note that Gill notes the ephah, holding above a bushel, is put for all measures, which should be alike and not different. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the ban on a great and a small measure. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the divers measures — a great and a small; the in thine house — the means of cheating in quantity; the every fraud covered — no loophole for deceit; the thorough honesty — honest in the sight of all (2 Cor. 8:21).

The witnesses agree the rule reaches from weights to measures, from the bag to the house: as no false weights, so no "divers measures" — a large to receive and a small to give out; the doubling of the command covers every means of cheating in quantity, that fraud find no loophole; the law thus binds the conscience to a thorough honesty in all dealings, leaving no instrument of deceit in house or bag; it teaches that the people of God are to be upright in every transaction, "providing for honest things, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of men" (2 Cor. 8:21).

So the witnesses make the verse no divers measures: The witnesses note the divers measures: a great and a small. The witnesses note the in thine house: the means of cheating in quantity. The witnesses note the every fraud covered: no loophole for deceit. The witnesses note the thorough honesty: honest in the sight of all (2 Cor. 8:21). The expositors observe the divers measures: a great and a small. The expositors observe the in thine house: the means of cheating in quantity. The expositors observe the every fraud covered: no loophole for deceit. The expositors observe the thorough honesty: honest in the sight of all (2 Cor. 8:21). The believer is taught to mark the divers measures, the in thine house, the every fraud covered, the thorough honesty, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that no differing measures were to be kept — the ban on fraud reaching from weights to measures, the conscience bound to a thorough honesty in every dealing, honest in the sight of all (2 Cor. 8:21).
15:
"But thou shalt have a perfect and just weight, a perfect and just measure shalt thou have: that thy days may be lengthened in the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee." The witnesses note the positive command — to keep perfect and just weights and measures, with the promise of long days. The witnesses note that Gill notes full weights and full measures, alike and according to the standard of the country. The witnesses note that the promise was that their days be lengthened in the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a perfect and just weight — full and true; the a just measure — according to the standard; the thy days lengthened — the promise on honesty; the integrity blessed — the just man's children blessed (Prov. 20:7).

The witnesses agree honesty in dealing is here both commanded and rewarded: the people were to have "a perfect and just weight" and measure — full and true to the standard — and to this plain fairness God attaches the promise that "thy days may be lengthened"; the joining of long life to honest trade shows how God blesses uprightness and how integrity is the way of welfare; it teaches that righteousness in common business is no small matter but a thing God honours with his favour, "the just man walketh in his integrity: his children are blessed after him" (Prov. 20:7), honesty rewarded with abiding good.

So the witnesses make the verse a just weight: The witnesses note the a perfect and just weight: full and true. The witnesses note the a just measure: according to the standard. The witnesses note the thy days lengthened: the promise on honesty. The witnesses note the integrity blessed: the just man's children blessed (Prov. 20:7). The expositors observe the a perfect and just weight: full and true. The expositors observe the a just measure: according to the standard. The expositors observe the thy days lengthened: the promise on honesty. The expositors observe the integrity blessed: the just man's children blessed (Prov. 20:7). The believer is taught to mark the a perfect and just weight, the a just measure, the thy days lengthened, the integrity blessed, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that they were to keep perfect and just weights and measures, that their days be lengthened — honesty commanded and rewarded, God honouring integrity in common trade with his favour (Prov. 20:7).
16:
"For all that do such things, and all that do unrighteously, are an abomination unto the LORD thy God." The witnesses note that all who deal falsely with weights and measures are an abomination to the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes those who keep different weights and measures and use them to defraud their neighbours in buying and selling. The witnesses note that all who do unrighteously are an abomination to God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all that do such things — the dishonest in trade; the all that do unrighteously — every wrong-dealing; the an abomination — hateful to the LORD; the unjust gain loathsome — riches not by right (Jer. 17:11).

The witnesses agree the verse brands all dishonest dealing as hateful to God: "all that do such things, and all that do unrighteously, are an abomination unto the LORD"; the strong word shows that fraud in trade is no mere worldly fault but a sin God abhors, a thing as offensive to him as idolatry; the reaching of "all that do unrighteously" beyond weights to every kind of wrong-dealing makes plain that God's holiness extends to the marketplace, and that the unjust gain men prize is loathsome in his sight, "he that getteth riches, and not by right, shall leave them" (Jer. 17:11), unrighteousness an abomination he will judge.

So the witnesses make the verse an abomination: The witnesses note the all that do such things: the dishonest in trade. The witnesses note the all that do unrighteously: every wrong-dealing. The witnesses note the an abomination: hateful to the LORD. The witnesses note the unjust gain loathsome: riches not by right (Jer. 17:11). The expositors observe the all that do such things: the dishonest in trade. The expositors observe the all that do unrighteously: every wrong-dealing. The expositors observe the an abomination: hateful to the LORD. The expositors observe the unjust gain loathsome: riches not by right (Jer. 17:11). The believer is taught to mark the all that do such things, the all that do unrighteously, the an abomination, the unjust gain loathsome, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that all who deal falsely are an abomination to the LORD — fraud no mere worldly fault but a sin God abhors, his holiness reaching to the marketplace, unjust gain loathsome in his sight (Jer. 17:11).
17:
"Remember what Amalek did unto thee by the way, when ye were come forth out of Egypt;" The witnesses note the charge to remember the unprovoked assault of Amalek on Israel after the Exodus. The witnesses note that Gill notes how the Amalekites came out against them and fought at Rephidim. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the Amalekites were the first to make a hostile attack on the people and interrupt their journey, and recalls God's sentence against them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the remember Amalek — the unprovoked assault; the by the way — after the Exodus; the the first to attack — open enmity against God's people; the the enemy of the church — the LORD's war with Amalek (Exod. 17:16).

The witnesses agree the command to "remember Amalek" keeps alive the memory of a peculiar wickedness: Amalek was the first to assault Israel after the Exodus, attacking the redeemed people of God without cause as they journeyed; Calvin recalls the divine sentence pronounced against them for it; the bidding to remember marks both the heinousness of the deed — open enmity against God's people — and the certainty of his justice, that wrong done to his own is not forgotten by him; Amalek stands as a type of the implacable enemy of God and his church, the flesh and the world warring against the redeemed (cf. Exod. 17:16, the LORD will have war with Amalek).

So the witnesses make the verse remember Amalek: The witnesses note the remember Amalek: the unprovoked assault. The witnesses note the by the way: after the Exodus. The witnesses note the the first to attack: open enmity against God's people. The witnesses note the the enemy of the church: the LORD's war with Amalek (Exod. 17:16). The expositors observe the remember Amalek: the unprovoked assault. The expositors observe the by the way: after the Exodus. The expositors observe the the first to attack: open enmity against God's people. The expositors observe the the enemy of the church: the LORD's war with Amalek (Exod. 17:16). The believer is taught to mark the remember Amalek, the by the way, the the first to attack, the the enemy of the church, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that they were to remember Amalek's unprovoked assault — a peculiar wickedness against the redeemed, Amalek a type of the implacable enemy of God's church, with whom the LORD has war (Exod. 17:16).
18:
"How he met thee by the way, and smote the hindmost of thee, even all that were feeble behind thee, when thou wast faint and weary; and he feared not God." The witnesses note the cruelty of Amalek — falling on the feeble and weary stragglers, without fear of God. The witnesses note that Gill notes he met them not with provisions, as humanity would, but sword in hand to stop their journey and make them captives. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him smiting the hindmost, the feeble and faint. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the smote the hindmost — the stragglers cut off; the the feeble and weary — preying on the weakest; the when thou wast faint — at their lowest; the he feared not God — the root of his cruelty (Gen. 20:11).

The witnesses agree the deed was marked by a special cowardice and cruelty: Amalek "smote the hindmost," the feeble and weary who lagged behind, preying on the weakest when Israel was faint — and "he feared not God"; the picking-off of the helpless reveals a heart void of pity and of the fear of God, the root of all such cruelty; the law brands this contempt of God shown in cruelty to the weak, teaching that the fear of God is the spring of mercy, and its absence the source of oppression — the want of God's fear leaving men capable of any inhumanity (cf. Gen. 20:11, surely the fear of God is not in this place).

So the witnesses make the verse smote the feeble: The witnesses note the smote the hindmost: the stragglers cut off. The witnesses note the the feeble and weary: preying on the weakest. The witnesses note the when thou wast faint: at their lowest. The witnesses note the he feared not God: the root of his cruelty (Gen. 20:11). The expositors observe the smote the hindmost: the stragglers cut off. The expositors observe the the feeble and weary: preying on the weakest. The expositors observe the when thou wast faint: at their lowest. The expositors observe the he feared not God: the root of his cruelty (Gen. 20:11). The believer is taught to mark the smote the hindmost, the the feeble and weary, the when thou wast faint, the he feared not God, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that Amalek smote the feeble and weary stragglers, fearing not God — a special cowardice and cruelty, the want of God's fear the root of his inhumanity to the weak (Gen. 20:11).
19:
"Therefore it shall be, when the LORD thy God hath given thee rest from all thine enemies... thou shalt blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven; thou shalt not forget it." The witnesses note the command to blot out Amalek when Israel had rest — the enemy of God's people doomed. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was when they had rest from all their enemies round about, not only the Canaanites but all neighbouring nations. The witnesses note that they were to blot out the remembrance of Amalek and not forget. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the when God gives rest — the deferred judgment; the blot out the remembrance — the enemy utterly destroyed; the forget it not — the sure purpose of God; the the foe's final doom — war with Amalek forever (Exod. 17:16).

The witnesses agree the doom of Amalek was certain, though deferred to the time of rest: when God had given Israel peace from all their foes, they were to "blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven" — the implacable enemy utterly destroyed; the deferring of the judgment to a settled time shows God's patience and his sure purpose, that wrong against his people is repaid in his season; and the total blotting-out of Amalek pictures the final overthrow of all the enemies of God and his church, the doom of those who, fearing not God, war against his redeemed — "the LORD hath sworn... the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation" (Exod. 17:16).

So the witnesses make the verse blot out Amalek: The witnesses note the when God gives rest: the deferred judgment. The witnesses note the blot out the remembrance: the enemy utterly destroyed. The witnesses note the forget it not: the sure purpose of God. The witnesses note the the foe's final doom: war with Amalek forever (Exod. 17:16). The expositors observe the when God gives rest: the deferred judgment. The expositors observe the blot out the remembrance: the enemy utterly destroyed. The expositors observe the forget it not: the sure purpose of God. The expositors observe the the foe's final doom: war with Amalek forever (Exod. 17:16). The believer is taught to mark the when God gives rest, the blot out the remembrance, the forget it not, the the foe's final doom, and to deal justly and mercifully. The verse thus records that Amalek was to be blotted out when Israel had rest — the implacable enemy's certain doom, picturing the final overthrow of all the foes of God's church, the LORD's war with Amalek from generation to generation (Exod. 17:16).
